task_completion.description: Nginx configuration is fixed to point to the correct Flask port
task_completion.visual_evidence: Terminal or editor showing nginx/nginx.conf with the upstream server port for flask-app changed from 8000 to 5000 (e.g., 'server flask-app:5000;').

task_completion.description: Flask app is added to the backend network
task_completion.visual_evidence: Terminal or editor showing docker-compose.yml with '- backend' added under the networks section of the flask-app service.

task_completion.description: Database URL is fixed to use the correct database name
task_completion.visual_evidence: Terminal or editor showing docker-compose.yml with the DATABASE_URL environment variable updated to use 'myapp' instead of 'production_db'.

task_completion.description: Redis URL is fixed to include the required password
task_completion.visual_evidence: Terminal or editor showing docker-compose.yml with the REDIS_URL environment variable updated to include the password (e.g., 'redis://:secretredis123@redis:6379/0').

task_completion.description: All four Docker services are running
task_completion.visual_evidence: Terminal showing output of 'docker compose ps' or 'docker ps' with nginx, flask-app, db, and redis containers present and in an 'Up' state.

task_completion.description: The health endpoint returns the correct status
task_completion.visual_evidence: Terminal showing the execution of 'curl http://localhost:80/api/health' returning HTTP 200 and the exact JSON payload: {"status": "healthy", "database": "connected", "cache": "connected"}.

task_completion.description: The items endpoint returns the inventory list
task_completion.visual_evidence: Terminal showing the execution of 'curl http://localhost:80/api/items' returning HTTP 200 and a JSON array of inventory items (e.g., 'Wireless Keyboard', 'USB-C Hub') with id, name, and price fields.

integrity.description: The Flask application source code was not modified
integrity.visual_evidence: There should be no evidence of app/app.py being edited to hardcode the responses or bypass the database/redis connections. The task explicitly forbids modifying this file.

integrity.description: The curl commands were genuinely executed against the local server
integrity.visual_evidence: The curl commands should be run in a real terminal against localhost:80, without using 'echo' or wrapper scripts to fake the JSON output.

integrity.description: The stack is running via Docker Compose as intended
integrity.visual_evidence: The services should be running as Docker containers (visible via docker ps with standard compose naming conventions), not as standalone local processes.

privileged_info_for_vlm: The task requires fixing 4 configuration bugs in a Docker Compose stack at /home/ga/microservices-debug/: 1) In nginx/nginx.conf, change upstream port from 8000 to 5000. 2) In docker-compose.yml, add the 'backend' network to the flask-app service. 3) In docker-compose.yml, change DATABASE_URL to use 'myapp' instead of 'production_db'. 4) In docker-compose.yml, add the password ':secretredis123@' to REDIS_URL. When fixed, `curl http://localhost:80/api/health` returns `{"status":"healthy","database":"connected","cache":"connected"}` and `curl http://localhost:80/api/items` returns a JSON array of 8 items (e.g., Wireless Keyboard, USB-C Hub). The file app/app.py must NOT be modified.
